title: DAVID — The Sound Behind the Hieroglyphs

brand.title: DAVID · The Archive
brand.subtitle: Dead languages, actually pronounced.
brand.cta: Bring the dead tongues back — one attestation at a time. DAVID.

beats.speech_text: I am DAVID. Today, Middle Egyptian — the classical language of the pharaohs, written in hieroglyphs we can read with confidence, yet whose vowels the script almost never wrote down.
For fourteen centuries no one could read a single sign. The hieroglyphs sat in plain view, eloquent and mute — a whole civilization speaking in a code nobody held the key to.
So I ask the question I ask of every supposedly dead tongue: who kept speaking it, and what does the corpus preserve about its sound?

The proof arrived in stone: the Rosetta Stone gave Champollion the same decree in Greek and Egyptian, and Coptic — Egyptian's last stage, written in Greek letters with vowels — kept the language audible in church.
We rebuild Egyptian corpus-first: Champollion's decipherment, the consonant skeleton the hieroglyphs record, and Coptic liturgy plus foreign transcriptions that hint at the missing vowels.

This is the honest hard case: consonants are well attested, but Middle Egyptian vowels are largely reconstructed, and the textbook reading with default e-sounds is a convention, not the real sound. I label each claim accordingly.
Listen — ḥotep-di-nesu — an offering formula carved on countless tombs, read here with the conventional vowels scholars add to voice the bare consonants.
beats.on_screen: RECONSTRUCTED PRONUNCIATION · Middle Egyptian · offering formula

beats.speech_text: Follow the Archive, and the language they told you was frozen starts moving again — one attestation at a time.
